text: Abstract This study aims to provide complementary information on the plasma protein corona surrounding hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules, surface functionalized with different ligands, and shed light on the long-standing debate concerning the “hard” vs “soft” protein corona distinction. Through sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and protein quantification assays, hard corona information was obtained, which showed a similar adsorption pattern for all the nanocapsules but depletion of human serum albumin (HSA). However, through isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and dynamic light scattering (DLS), performed in situ, the adsorption of a high amount of HSA with low binding affinity, pointed out its role as a major component of the soft corona. Meanwhile, apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) shows high binding affinities, but low stoichiometry values, confirming its presence in the hard corona. The composition of the protein corona seems to greatly depend on the method used to isolate and analyze it. Therefore, it is essential to apply multiple approaches to determine the biological identity of nanoparticles in order to predict their behavior in biological media and ensure a successful therapeutic application.
